title: Whittled lime toggle
subtitle: Small oval wooden toggle or button for bags and cord closures
excerpt: A small oval wooden toggle in seasoned lime, 4 cm long with two through-holes for cord attachment. A very short beginner project that uses only the push cut and thumb-pivot cut on a small symmetrical form.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on small treen fittings. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry on small turned and carved wooden fittings.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber at a stable moisture content. Dimensionally stable once at equilibrium with the room.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short controlled cut pivoted by the off-hand thumb on the back of the blade. Used for fine shaping where blade-travel is small.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard.

content.text: A toggle is a short tapered cylinder or oval with two holes bored through it for cord attachment. It closes a bag, secures a duvet cover, or replaces a lost button. Making one from a short off-cut of
seasoned

lime takes twenty minutes and uses the push cut only. Lime is the softest hardwood in the UK native canon; it is the preferred relief-carving wood for exactly this reason. The grain is fine and even, and the surface takes a knife smoothly from any direction.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

A seasoned lime off-cut: 5 cm long, 2.5 cm wide, 1.5 cm deep. The finished toggle is 4 cm long and 2 cm wide; the extra length and width provides grip during shaping. Beech is a good substitute, slightly harder. Sycamore works well.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
; a brace and bit for the cord holes (use a 6 mm bit); a leather strop; a small amount of raw linseed oil.
Method
Mark the oval outline on the flat face of the blank: 4 cm long, 2 cm wide, with an even rounded taper at each end.
Carve the profile to the outline with
push cuts
. Work the taper at each end with
thumb-pivot cuts
. The cross-section is a rounded oblong: 15 mm wide, 10 mm thick at the center, tapering to 8 mm at each end.
Smooth the surface by working around the perimeter with light push cuts, each overlapping the last by half. The surface should be even enough that no facets catch light.

Mark the two cord holes 1 cm from each end, centered on the width. Bore with the brace and bit. Hold the blank in a vise or clamp it between bench dogs before boring; the blank is too small to hold in the hand while boring through.
Clean the hole edges with a light push cut around each hole opening.
Finishing

Sand through 180 and 240 grit. Apply raw linseed oil; wipe off the surplus after fifteen minutes. The toggle is not food-contact; cure time is not critical, but let it dry for 2 days before attaching to cord.
Variations
Longer toggle for a heavier bag: 5 cm long, 2.5 cm wide. Use a 8 mm cord hole.
Chip-carved decorative panel: add a small chip-carved triangle or diamond on the flat face before boring the holes.
Left-handed version: the toggle is symmetrical; no adjustment needed.
Care
A toggle on daily outdoor use benefits from re-oiling every 6 months. Wipe with a little raw linseed, let dry for a day.

items.symptom: Cord hole splits the wood at the end
items.cause: Hole bored too close to the end-grain
items.fix: The hole center must be at least 8 mm from the end. Move the hole inward by 2 mm if the blank shows any sign of splitting toward the end.

items.cause: Push cuts taken at different depths across adjacent facets
items.fix: Work around the full perimeter before checking the surface. A single pass around makes a 16-sided polygon; two passes makes near-round.
